did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering, an unsupervised machine learning algorithm, for skin lesion segmentation in melanoma detection using non-dermoscopic images. It employs histogram-based automatic cluster selection and entropy analysis of HSV color channels to segment melanoma lesions from normal skin.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new methodology for skin lesion segmentation using Fuzzy C-Means clustering and histogram-based cluster selection. It details a specific algorithm, describes the implementation on the UMCG dataset (170 images), and reports quantitative results (95.69% accuracy) with comparative analysis against existing methods, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Fuzzy C-Means Clustering with Histogram based Cluster Selection for Skin Lesion Segmentation using Non-Dermoscopic Images
journal: International Journal of Interactive Multimedia and Artificial Intelligence
brief_summary: This study proposes an unsupervised skin lesion segmentation method for melanoma detection using non-dermoscopic images to address the challenges of non-uniform illumination and low contrast. The methodology employs Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering with automatic cluster selection based on histogram local maxima and Euclidean distance properties. To optimize segmentation, the entropy of Hue, Saturation, and Value color channels was analyzed, identifying the Value channel as the most effective for clustering. The system was evaluated on the University Medical Center Groningen (UMCG) dataset, which comprised 170 images of melanoma and benign naevi. Quantitative results demonstrated that the proposed method achieved a segmentation accuracy of 95.69%, sensitivity of 90.02%, and specificity of 99.15%. Comparative analysis indicated that the proposed approach outperformed existing methods, including Otsu thresholding, in segmenting lesion regions from normal skin.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for segmentation. The UMCG dataset contained 70 melanoma images. The study aims to segment melanoma lesions from normal skin using non-dermoscopic images.

Used as a comparator class in the UMCG dataset (100 images) to distinguish from melanoma. The paper notes that classification between melanoma and benign naevi is a future goal, while the current work focuses on segmentation of lesions from normal skin.
The background class against which lesions are segmented. The histogram analysis compared normal skin, melanoma skin, and benign naevus skin to determine cluster selection thresholds.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion region from normal skin
all_task_types.details: The paper performs unsupervised skin lesion segmentation using Fuzzy C-Means clustering on non-dermoscopic images. The task involves classifying pixels as either 'lesion' or 'normal skin' to produce a segmentation mask. The authors explicitly state: 'The segmentation has been done as a classification problem. Here, the pixels in the images are classified as lesion or normal skin pixels.' However, the overall task type is segmentation, not diagnostic classification of the image as a whole.

datasets_used.name: UMCG Dataset (University Medical Center Groningen)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of non-dermoscopic skin lesion images captured using a Nikon D3 or Nikon D1x camera body with a Nikkor 2.8/105 mm micro lens at a distance of 33 cm. The images were sourced from the Department of Dermatology of the University Medical Center Groningen.

use.training: Used for experimental analysis and segmentation; however, the method is unsupervised (Fuzzy C-Means) and does not use a traditional training phase with labeled data for model weights.
use.validation: Used for qualitative and quantitative performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) against ground truth masks.
use.testing: Used for testing the segmentation performance of the proposed method compared to Otsu_R.
use.other: Used for histogram analysis to determine cluster selection thresholds by analyzing the distribution of normal skin, melanoma skin, and benign naevus skin.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using segmentation accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, and compared qualitatively and quantitatively against Otsu thresholding without statistical significance testing.

UMCG Dataset.notes: The proposed method outperformed the Otsu_R method by a sensitivity of 11.3%, specificity of 12.78%, and accuracy of 12.18%. The Value channel of the HSV color space was selected for segmentation due to having higher entropy compared to Hue and Saturation channels. The threshold for cluster selection was set at 5671 pixels based on Euclidean distance analysis of histogram local maxima.

architecture_used.main_model: Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) Clustering with Histogram-based Cluster Selection

architecture_details.channel_selection_method: Entropy analysis of Hue, Saturation, and Value channels; Value channel selected due to highest entropy
architecture_details.cluster_selection_method: Automatic selection based on histogram local maxima and Euclidean distance
architecture_details.cluster_threshold: 5671 pixels (Euclidean distance threshold between two prominent local maxima)
architecture_details.distribution_detection: Binomial distribution (2 clusters) if distance <= 5671; Normal distribution (Normal Skin) if distance > 5671
architecture_details.post_processing: Morphological filtering (opening) to remove unwanted artifacts

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Segmentation mask (binary classification of pixels as lesion or normal skin)

preprocessing_techniques: Color space conversion from RGB to HSV
Contrast enhancement applied to the Value channel
Morphological filtering (opening) to remove unwanted artifacts
conclusions: The proposed method effectively segments melanoma lesion regions from normal skin in non-dermoscopic images using Fuzzy C-Means clustering with automatic cluster selection based on histogram properties.
The Value channel of the HSV color space was identified as the most effective for segmentation due to having higher entropy compared to the Hue and Saturation channels.
The system achieved a segmentation accuracy of 95.69%, sensitivity of 90.02%, and specificity of 99.15% on the UMCG dataset.
The proposed method outperformed the Otsu_R thresholding method, showing improvements in sensitivity (11.3%), specificity (12.78%), and accuracy (12.18%).

Automatic cluster selection was successfully implemented by analyzing the Euclidean distance between the two most prominent local maxima in the histogram; a threshold of 5671 pixels was determined to distinguish between binomial distribution (lesion) and normal distribution (normal skin).
The method addresses challenges associated with non-dermoscopic images, such as non-uniform illumination and low contrast, by utilizing contrast enhancement and morphological filtering.
The study acknowledges that while segmentation is effective, the classification of melanoma from benign naevi remains a future goal.
important_remarks: The study is limited to segmentation of lesions from normal skin; it does not perform classification between melanoma and benign naevi, which the authors identify as a future goal.
The method is specifically designed for non-dermoscopic images and may not be directly applicable to dermoscopic images due to different image properties, requiring customization for such use cases.
The dataset (UMCG) is small (170 images total: 70 melanoma, 100 naevus), which limits the generalizability and statistical robustness of the findings.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparative results against the Otsu_R method.

The cluster selection threshold (5671 pixels) was determined empirically by analyzing the Euclidean distance between local maxima in histograms of normal, melanoma, and benign naevus skin, which may introduce bias if the distribution of these classes varies in other populations.

The hardware specifications (Intel Core i3, 4GB RAM) and runtime (1.7 seconds per image) suggest the method is computationally lightweight but may not reflect performance on high-resolution clinical datasets or mobile deployment constraints.
The ground truth masks were created manually (as shown in Fig. 4), introducing potential inter-observer variability that is not quantified or discussed.
